fact: September 5 is exactly 248 days into the year, which means there are only 117 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime spider season - house spiders are frantically searching for mates and that's why you suddenly see them everywhere indoors!
The sun is setting about 2-3 minutes earlier each day around September 5, making the evenings noticeably shorter as we race toward the autumn equinox.
Monarch butterflies are typically in full migration mode around this time, with some flying over 2,000 miles from Canada to Mexico - that's like flying from New York to Denver!
September 5 falls in the middle of Virgo season, making it one of the most 'Virgo-ish' days of the entire year for astrology fans.
In many northern regions, the first maple leaves often start changing color around this date, beginning their transformation from green to brilliant red and orange.
Crickets chirp slower as temperatures drop in early September - you can actually estimate the temperature by counting their chirps and doing simple math!
The moon rises about 50 minutes later each night around this time of year, creating dramatically different moonrise times from day to day.
September 5 is typically peak harvest time for apples in many northern orchards, when the fruit has the perfect balance of sugar and tartness.
In the Southern Hemisphere, September 5 marks the heart of late winter, when many animals are preparing for spring by growing new fur or feathers.
The Atlantic hurricane season usually reaches its peak around September 5 - this time period sees more tropical storm activity than most other times of the year.
Squirrels go absolutely nuts around this time in northern climates, frantically burying acorns and nuts for winter - a single squirrel can bury thousands of nuts in just a few weeks!
